title: Data Visualization
concept: Charts turn numbers into pictures the brain grasps instantly. The right chart reveals patterns; the wrong one can mislead.

exercises.prompt: Recap: EDA means exploring data to…?
exercises.explain: Right — get to know the data first.

cards.title: Your brain loves pictures
cards.body: 10,000 numbers = a blur. One good chart = instant understanding.

cards.title: What is data visualization?
cards.body: Turning data into visual form — charts, graphs, maps — so patterns jump out at you.

cards.title: Pick the right chart
cards.body: Bar: compare categories · Line: change over time · Pie: parts of a whole · Scatter: relationship between two things.

cards.title: You already use it
cards.body: Spotify Wrapped, your fitness app's graphs, weather trend lines, business dashboards — all data visualization.

cards.title: Charts can mislead
cards.body: A chopped-off axis or a flashy 3D pie can distort the truth. Good visualization is honest visualization.

exercises.prompt: Best chart to show a value changing over time?
exercises.explain: Line charts are made for trends over time.

options.text: Patterns are far easier to see as a picture than as raw numbers

exercises.explain: Visualization makes patterns pop that would be invisible in a table.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a misleading axis can make a chart distort the truth.
exercises.explain: True — how you draw a chart can mislead. Honest scales matter.

exercises.sentence: To compare sales across 5 cities, a ___ chart works well.
exercises.explain: Bar charts are great for comparing values across categories like cities.

exercises.prompt: You want to show whether taller people tend to weigh more (two numbers per person). Best chart?
exercises.explain: A scatter plot shows the relationship between two numbers per item — perfect for height vs weight (we'll call this 'correlation' next unit).
